Getting Bombarded with Questions at the HexSchool Alumni Meetup
On 2019/09/29 (Teacher's Day), HexSchool held an alumni meetup. There was free chat time during the event, so this post records the process of me getting bombarded with student questions (?!).
A bit of preamble about the HexSchool alumni meetup~
HexSchool held a meetup on Teacher's Day. At first I was worried turnout would be low because of the weather shift, but turnout was actually pretty solid!
About 1XX people — actually I forget the exact number. I didn't supplement the photos later either.
The meetup also invited five alumni to come back and share their job-search, interview, and learning takeaways with the attendees. The content was super solid.
Of course there were snacks — sadly I didn't take many photos. So enjoy these two:

Later there was a chat segment, which was the main point of the meetup. You could meet the teachers, TAs, or alumni you'd been wanting to talk to and have them share their energy with you. I'd planned to hide in a corner and slack off on my phone (yay).
But I was wrong. I immediately got

In the moment, my goldfish brain might've made me misspeak or struggle to explain things, so I decided to organize the questions and the advice I gave. Hopefully this helps people who wanted to come but couldn't and want to ask me similar stuff.
Since the askers came and went, I don't remember everyone, so I won't name names. Some questions might be missing too because there were a lot (goldfish brain confirmed).
Student: I'm going through "JavaScript Apprentice's Trial" right now. I can do the small exercises, but the moment I try a big project I get stuck — sometimes I can't finish at all.
Me: This is mostly because you
(smartass answer). Honestly, I had the same problem early on with PHP. When I wanted to build a project, my brain would stall. Eventually I realized the issue was that
I wasn't breaking the features apart
. What do I mean? Say you want to build a feature, and that feature has 4 sub-features: A, B, C, D. You're probably thinking about

— that's the main problem. You should split features out and only think about one at a time. So the right approach is: think about how to build A, finish A, then think about B, and so on. That way you know what to build, and your self-confidence doesn't take a hit either.
Student: I set a goal but I can't hit it. In a situation like this, would you recommend adjusting the goal, or keeping it and gathering more info before pushing toward the original?
Me: Quick question — anyone here started learning Vue or seen my RO Online (Ragnarok Online) e-commerce project?

Me: Most people have? (Surprised.) Honestly, when I first started the Vue assignment, my goal was to build the RO e-commerce using Canvas. But I realized I just don't know Canvas, and learning Canvas + building a product in the time I had was an impossible mission. So I adjusted the goal — used the skills I did have to build a compromise version, while still trying to recreate the in-game look as closely as possible. So my project ended up with a noticeable difference in look between the Vue and Vue 2 versions, but I got way closer to my original goal. So when you find you can't hit your goal, my advice is to try adjusting it slightly. You can keep the original goal if you want, but I personally don't recommend it — when you keep missing, your confidence and momentum erode. Simply put: you can't demand perfection, but you can demand "better". My project is a reference for that. Just my take though — adjust based on your own situation.
How do I build connections with experts?
Student: I attend a lot of conferences and meetups, but I can never quite talk to the experts there. How do I build connections with them?
Me: Easy. Lots of ways. You could just send them a Facebook friend request directly (super-serious smartass answer).
Me: If you really want to build a connection — or even a weak tie — try

. Not deliberately, but don't fear them. Try participating in stuff. For example, this Ironman challenge I signed up for two topics — a backend one and a frontend JavaScript-beginner one. I already know some core concepts, but there are still bugs in my posts. Those bugs ended up bringing two experts into my comment section, and I grew from their corrections.

Me: As long as you're willing to publicly try writing and accept your own mistakes, you have a high chance of bumping into these experts. Another path: study what they've shared. Post your thoughts in the comments. For example, Huli shared the game
recently — I thought it was super fun. So I DM'd Huli privately asking if I could write a walkthrough post. Out of respect, I told him I'd show him the post before publishing.
Walkthrough: How I Escaped Huli's r3:0 Otherworld

Me: Through that you've built a weak tie. Many ways exist. You can also attend HexSchool's "Expert visits HexSchool" events (product placement). If you speak up often there, the speakers will gradually remember you — same way you all remember me. Another way to build a connection.
Student: I don't know how to negotiate salary with interviewers properly.

Me: I'm not great at giving the right advice here — I personally don't prioritize salary that much. In Kaohsiung, my floor is around NT$30,000. In northern Taiwan, around NT$40,000. But generally, if you don't want to get hit too hard in negotiation, ask for slightly more than your target so you have wiggle room. If you want NT$40,000, ask for NT$42,000 — that gives you NT$2,000 of slack. Some companies might cut you down to NT$38,000, but most companies adjust back to your real target after the 3-month probation. So there's not much difference in the end. I personally don't weigh salary heavily — my first priority is growth opportunity. So I'd set salary at a survival baseline. That way, if you get laid off or want to switch jobs, you can present your current dev capability at the next salary level.
Student: I don't feel challenged in my current job. My growth feels too smooth. I feel like I'm not growing anymore.

Me: First, a baseline concept: companies actually constrain your tech growth, unless they're constantly building new systems or it's a startup that needs you to ship something new. Otherwise it's mostly maintenance. Maintenance prioritizes stability, so they're not going to constantly push you to upgrade versions. If you want challenge, the simplest move is to switch companies. But switching usually comes with a pain period — if your tech depth isn't strong enough yet, I wouldn't recommend switching. I'd recommend learning mainstream tech after work so when something happens, your tech is solid. Maintaining existing projects has upsides too — your learning curve is gentler, since most problems are Googleable. If you pick something super trendy, the learning curve gets steeper because resources are thinner.

I was bombarded to the point of nearly losing my voice. I can't give substantive help, but I tried to give some advice. Some students told me they didn't get to ask because there were too many people around me. So if there's anything else you want to chat about, feel free to DM me privately. Just expect slower replies (heh).
I'm Ray. See you at the next HexSchool alumni meetup
